tree_title: Contact Lenses: Types and How They Work
overall_topic: Contact Lenses: Types and How They Work
questions: When researching contact lenses and eye health, patients are often advised to get an annual eye exam at a major U.S. academic medical center known for its comprehensive specialty care. Focusing on this same institution, identify (1) its telehealth-based second-opinion program that allows patients to receive expert review without traveling, and (2) its centralized resource that provides a complete guide to all of its departments, institutes, and services. For the telehealth program, describe how it works (including key steps), pricing structure for U.S. and international patients, insurance considerations, and state or country availability limitations. Then explain the purpose and scope of the comprehensive guide resource and how it supports patients seeking specialized care such as vision or contact lens services.

rounds.question: When researching contact lenses and eye health, patients are often advised to get an annual eye exam at a major U.S. academic medical center known for its comprehensive specialty care. Focusing on this same institution, identify (1) its telehealth-based second-opinion program that allows patients to receive expert review without traveling, and (2) its centralized resource that provides a complete guide to all of its departments, institutes, and services. For the telehealth program, describe how it works (including key steps), pricing structure for U.S. and international patients, insurance considerations, and state or country availability limitations. Then explain the purpose and scope of the comprehensive guide resource and how it supports patients seeking specialized care such as vision or contact lens services.
checklist_width: Telehealth second-opinion program identified by name
Explanation of the three main steps (registration/intake, records collection and matching, expert written report with optional virtual visit)
Pricing for U.S. patients (written report only and report + virtual visit)
Insurance/Medicare coverage limitations and self-pay details
State availability distinctions and countries where service is unavailable
Identification of the comprehensive guide to departments/institutes/services
Explanation of the guide’s purpose and scope (covers all departments, institutes, and services)
checklist_depth: Virtual Second Opinions by Cleveland Clinic (delivered by The Clinic, joint venture with Amwell) + tied to the same institution known for annual eye exams
Comprehensive guide to all departments, institutes and services within Cleveland Clinic + clearly linked to the same healthcare organization
rounds.rationale: The question uses the clue of receiving an annual eye exam at a well-known academic medical center (deep logic anchor) to lead the searcher to the correct institution without directly naming the target programs. It then requires broad aggregation of details about two distinct resources from that same organization—a virtual second-opinion telehealth service and a comprehensive departmental guide—demanding comparison of procedures, costs, eligibility, and scope (wide information retrieval).
source_nodes: Virtual Second Opinions | Online Services | Cleveland Clinic
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Virtual Second Opinions Learn More Get Started (U.S.) Call: 216.444.3223 An easy and fast way to help you make a more informed, confident decision about your care. All from th
rounds.answer_a:
The institution in question is **Johns Hopkins Medicine**, a leading U.S. academic medical center renowned for comprehensive specialty care, including ophthalmology and contact lens services.

**(1) Telehealth Second-Opinion Program: Johns Hopkins Remote Second Opinions**

This program allows patients worldwide to receive expert diagnostic reviews without traveling to Baltimore [1]. Here is how it works:

**Key Steps:** Patients submit a request online, provide medical records and relevant imaging, and a Johns Hopkins specialist reviews the case. A detailed written report with findings and recommendations is delivered, typically within 10 business days [1].

**Pricing Structure:** For U.S. patients, the cost starts at approximately $565, while international patients pay around $950. Fees vary depending on case complexity and specialty [1][2].

**Insurance Considerations:** The service is generally not covered by insurance, though patients can submit receipts to their insurers for potential reimbursement. Payment is required upfront [1].

**Availability Limitations:** The program is available to patients in all 50 U.S. states and internationally. However, because it is a consultation rather than a treatment program, licensing restrictions do not apply in the same way as direct telehealth visits, which may be limited by state medical licensing laws [2].

**(2) Centralized Resource: Johns Hopkins Medicine Health Library / "All Departments and Services" Directory**

Johns Hopkins provides a **comprehensive A-to-Z directory of all departments, institutes, and services** through its website [3]. This centralized guide covers every clinical specialty—from ophthalmology and contact lens services to cardiology and oncology. It helps patients identify the right department, understand available treatments, find specialists, and navigate appointments. For patients seeking vision care or contact lens evaluations, the directory links directly to the Wilmer Eye Institute, ensuring streamlined access to specialized ophthalmologic expertise [3].

## References
1. Johns Hopkins Medicine. "Remote Second Opinions." https://www.hopkinsmedicine.org/patient-care/remote-second-opinions
2. Johns Hopkins Medicine. "Remote Second Opinions FAQ." https://www.hopkinsmedicine.org/patient-care/remote-second-opinions/faq
3. Johns Hopkins Medicine. "All Departments and Services." https://www.hopkinsmedicine.org/patient-care/find-a-department
rounds.answer_b:
The **Cleveland Clinic** is a foremost U.S. academic medical center fulfilling these requirements, known for its extensive specialty treatments, including comprehensive ophthalmic care.

Its remote review program, **Virtual Second Opinions** (operated via *The Clinic by Cleveland Clinic*), delivers expert consultations without requiring travel [1]. 
**How it works:** Patients register online and are assigned a dedicated Nurse Care Manager who efficiently gathers their medical records, imaging, and laboratory results. A specialized Cleveland Clinic physician then evaluates the case and delivers a detailed written report outlining findings and next steps [1].
**Pricing and Insurance:** The service costs $1,850 for both U.S. and international patients. Because the review is legally categorized as an educational consultation rather than a traditional medical service, it is strictly self-pay. Commercial health insurance, Medicare, and Medicaid do not cover the cost, though U.S. patients can utilize HSA or FSA funds [2].
**Availability Limitations:** The program is fully accessible across all 50 U.S. states. While available globally, international access faces restrictions in specific countries due to local legal parameters and telemedicine regulations [2].

The institution's centralized resource is its **Institutes, Departments & Centers** directory [3]. 
**Purpose and Scope:** This exhaustive digital portal maps the health system’s entire clinical footprint, detailing every multidisciplinary institute, specialized department, and specialized center [3].
**Supporting Specialized Care:** For patients requiring specific vision services—such as an annual eye wellness exam or specialized contact lens fittings—the directory routes them straight to the Cole Eye Institute. By centrally organizing dedicated provider profiles, subspecialty outlines, and direct scheduling tools, the directory eliminates the friction of navigating a massive academic network. This functional centralization ensures that patients seeking routine eye health maintenance or complex optical care seamlessly connect with the appropriate experts [3].

## References
[1] Cleveland Clinic. "Virtual Second Opinions." Cleveland Clinic Online Services. Available at: https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinions
[2] The Clinic by Cleveland Clinic. "Frequently Asked Questions." Available at: https://www.theclinicbyclevelandclinic.com/faq/
[3] Cleveland Clinic. "Institutes, Departments & Centers." Cleveland Clinic Medical Directory. Available at: https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A identifies Johns Hopkins Medicine, which is incorrect. The ground truth institution is Cleveland Clinic (Virtual Second Opinions delivered via The Clinic by Cleveland Clinic). Agent B correctly identifies Cleveland Clinic and its Virtual Second Opinions program. Thus, Agent A fails the DEEP check.

Next, Width/Completeness: Agent B correctly names the program, explains core workflow steps (registration, nurse-led records collection, physician review with written report), addresses pricing and self-pay/insurance limitations, and notes state and international availability restrictions. However, it omits key details such as the optional virtual visit add-on, detailed tiered pricing (report-only vs. report + visit), and specific country exclusions. So B has some WIDE gaps, but no clear factual hallucinations. Agent A, while structured well, provides detailed but irrelevant information due to selecting the wrong institution.

Finally, User Experience: Agent B is well formatted, clearly structured, and directly aligned with the queried institution. Agent A’s answer, despite decent formatting, fundamentally fails the user’s intent because it centers on the wrong medical center.

Because Agent B gets the core entity correct and Agent A fails Deep Logic, B is MUCH_BETTER under the rubric.
